abstract: This paper investigates the role of Alternative Influence Networks (AIN) in spreading Covid-19 problematic information on Italian Facebook during the first year of the pandemic (March 2020-March 2021). Using a dataset of 175,228 Facebook posts from covid-skeptic accounts, the authors identify and cluster public figures mentioned in the posts, analyze engagement patterns, and conduct content analysis on the most engaging posts. The study finds that Intellectual Dark Web (IDW) influencers and other public figures generate disproportionately high engagement relative to their volume, and that legacy media remains central to the circulation of problematic information through processes of remediation.
key_claims: Alternative Influence Networks (AIN) play a central role in linking digital and legacy media in the circulation of Covid-19 problematic information.

Hybridity in the contemporary media system operates not just through intertwined practices between social and legacy media, but crucially through remediation of expert/celebrity statements originally voiced in legacy media.
Posts mentioning IDW Influencers and other public figures exhibit shares-dominant engagement patterns indicative of hyperpartisan amplification behavior.
Legacy media outlets and journalists remain central nodes in amplifying covid-skeptic narratives, complicating fact-checking because verbatim reporting of false statements is technically accurate.
Remediating statements by experts and intellectuals is a particularly effective manipulation technique because it lends authoritative voice to misleading narratives and evades fact-checker scrutiny.
methods: Collected 175,228 Facebook posts via CrowdTangle from a previously identified cluster of Italian covid-skeptic Facebook pages and groups (March 2020-March 2021).

Extracted capitalized words from message, title, description, and image text fields; selected the top 2,000 most frequent and manually classified 254 proper nouns into five non-exclusive categories (Alternative Influencers, Doctors/Scientists/Experts, Journalists, Other Public Figures, Politicians).
Filtered out figures holding public/institutional roles, leaving 79 figures, and applied k-means cluster analysis to identify five emergent clusters.
Computed engagement descriptive statistics and a comments/shares ratio metric (ranging -1 to 1, following Giglietto et al. 2019) for each cluster to detect amplification vs. reframing dynamics.

Conducted deductive content analysis on a stratified random sample (N=150) drawn from the top 10% most-engaged posts, stratified by cluster and by three comments/shares ratio bands (mostly shared, balanced, mostly commented).
findings: K-means analysis surfaced five clusters: Politicians, Other Public Figures, Journalists, Doctors/Scientists/Experts, and IDW Influencers.
IDW Influencers had the lowest post frequency (1,649) but among the highest mean interactions (1,030.05) and the lowest (most shares-oriented) comments/shares ratio (-0.28).
Politicians had the highest post volume (9,879) but lower mean engagement; Journalists had the highest mean interactions (2,679.76) and the most comment-oriented ratio (0.16).

The ten Facebook pages publishing the most highly engaging posts are dominated by mainstream-adjacent journalists (Nicola Porro, Gianluigi Paragone) and legacy outlets (La Verità, Libero), with AIN micro-celebrities (Montanari, Messora) further down the list.

Content analysis showed posts frequently remediate quotes and clips from TV talk shows and legacy newspaper interviews to reinforce covid-skeptic, anti-immigration, anti-government, and conspiracy narratives (e.g., microchip/global tyranny themes).

Doctors/Scientists/Experts cluster posts often weaponized expert statements to support covid-skepticism rather than convey medical information, with critical/controversial figures (e.g., Galli, Capua) attracting more comments than shares.
contributions: Provides empirical mapping of the Italian Alternative Influence Network during the pandemic, identifying a distinct Intellectual Dark Web (IDW) sub-cluster.
Demonstrates the utility of the comments/shares ratio metric for distinguishing amplification vs. contestation dynamics across actor types.
Extends theorization of hybrid media systems by emphasizing remediation of expert/celebrity statements as a key mechanism for circulating problematic information.
Highlights a fact-checking loophole: faithful reporting of false statements by public figures evades standard veracity ratings, enabling laundering of misinformation through legacy media.
framing: The paper situates itself within hybrid media system theory (Chadwick) and scholarship on Alternative Influence Networks (Lewis), the Intellectual Dark Web (Kelsey), problematic information (Jack), and source hacking (Donovan & Friedberg). It engages debates on inter-media agenda setting, micro-celebrity, and the relationship between alternative/anti-establishment movements and legacy media, applying these frameworks to the Italian Covid-19 infodemic.
